prompt_text: Compare Retool and AppSmith for build internal dashboards and admin panels quickly
response:
For **quickly building internal dashboards and admin panels**, **Retool** generally wins on speed, polish, and enterprise features, while **Appsmith** wins on cost, openness, and flexibility (especially if you want to self‑host or customize heavily).[1][3][4]

Below is a focused comparison specifically for your use case.

---

## 1. Core fit for dashboards/admin panels

Both tools are built for this exact scenario: connect to data sources, drag‑and‑drop UI, add a bit of JS, and ship internal tools in hours instead of weeks.[1][3][4]

- **Retool**
  - Optimized for **fast, production‑grade internal tools** (dashboards, admin panels, workflow tools).[2][4]
  - Strong for **data‑heavy UIs** and multi‑data‑source dashboards.[3][4]
  - Best persona: **data analyst / backend dev at mid‑to‑large companies** needing to wire up multiple systems quickly.[4]

- **Appsmith**
  - Also focused on **internal tools, admin panels, dashboards, CRUD apps**.[1][5]
  - Better when you want more **custom business logic** and **deep control** over behavior.[4]
  - Best persona: **developer‑heavy teams** that are comfortable with code and want open‑source flexibility and self‑hosting.[1][2][4]

For “build dashboards/admin panels ASAP,” Retool is slightly more “out‑of‑the‑box fast”; Appsmith is slightly more “hackable and controllable.”

---

## 2. Speed of development & UX polish

- **Retool – faster out-of-the-box**
  - Very **polished UI**, large library of components (tables, charts, forms, modals, etc.).[1][3]
  - **70+ native connectors**; many enterprise systems supported directly.[3][4]
  - Better documentation and onboarding; considered the **most polished** among internal tool builders.[3][4]
  - Often recommended when you “need a functional data‑dense interface in an afternoon.”[4]

- **Appsmith – fast but a bit less polished**
  - Drag‑and‑drop builder with strong component set, but UI/UX is **less refined** than Retool.[1][3][5]
  - **25+ native connectors**; enough for common databases and APIs, but fewer deep enterprise integrations.[3]
  - Steeper learning curve, especially because it leans more on **client‑side JavaScript**.[3]
  - Still very fast once your team learns it; good demos show full CRUD apps in minutes.[5]

If “time‑to‑first‑dashboard” is critical and budget is less of an issue, Retool has the edge.[1][3][4]

---

## 3. Data sources & integrations

If your dashboards/admin panels span many systems, this matters a lot.

- **Retool**
  - **70+ connectors** out-of-the-box: major SQL/NoSQL DBs (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, DynamoDB, Oracle, etc.) plus many SaaS APIs (Stripe, Salesforce, HubSpot, etc.).[3]
  - Very strong fit for **enterprise environments** with heterogeneous data sources.[3][4]

- **Appsmith**
  - **25+ connectors**: supports major DBs (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, MS SQL, Redshift, DynamoDB, Snowflake, etc.) and popular APIs/SaaS (REST, GraphQL, Google Sheets, Salesforce, Twilio, Airtable, Notion, Supabase, Neon, etc.).[3][4]
  - For unsupported services, you can always use REST/GraphQL, but you may lose some convenience compared to Retool’s native connectors.[3][4]

If you need **lots of enterprise connectors** with minimal custom work, Retool is stronger.[3][4]  
If your stack is mostly **common DBs + REST/GraphQL**, Appsmith is usually enough.[3][4]

---

## 4. Pricing & total cost

For internal dashboards with multiple users, cost can be decisive.

- **Retool**
  - Proprietary, SaaS with free tier (limited users) plus paid plans.[3]
  - Business tier roughly **$65/user/month** (example: 10 users ≈ $650/mo).[3]
  - Self‑hosting still requires paid licenses.[3]

- **Appsmith**
  - **Open source**; **self‑hosted Community Edition is free** with no user limits.[3][5]
  - Cloud pricing significantly lower; example: Business plan ≈ **$15/user/month** (10 users ≈ $150/mo).[3][2]
  - Very attractive for **startups and SMB
